inheritance-for-shared-behavior.label: Inheritance for Shared Behavior
inheritance-for-shared-behavior.summary: Create subclasses that reuse a base class so related catalog items share common setup and behavior without duplicated code.

sketch0.title: Why use a base class for shared behavior?

sketch1.title: How subclasses add their own data with super()

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.title: Build a shared base class for catalog items
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.prompt: You are organizing a small library catalog. In `models/base_item.py`, complete the `CatalogItem` class so its constructor stores `title` and its `summary()` method returns `Item: <title>`. In `main.py`, create one `CatalogItem` with the title `Deep Work` and print its summary. This practice matters because it shows how one base class can hold behavior that later subclasses will reuse.
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.title: Use super() to reuse shared setup
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.prompt: You are adding books to the catalog app. `CatalogItem` is already provided in `models/base_item.py`. In `models/book.py`, complete the `Book` subclass so it inherits from `CatalogItem`, calls `super().__init__(title)`, and stores `author`. In `main.py`, create `Book("Deep Work", "Cal Newport")` and print the title on one line and the author on the next line. This practice matters because subclasses often need shared parent setup plus their own extra data.
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.hint: Read the task "Use super() to reuse shared setup" and identify the required result.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.title: Override label() in two subclasses
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.prompt: You are preparing labels for a media shelf. The base class `CatalogItem` is provided. In `models/book.py`, make `Book.label()` return `Book: <title>`. In `models/movie.py`, make `Movie.label()` return `Movie: <title>`. In `main.py`, create `Book("Dune")` and `Movie("Arrival")`, then print each label on its own line. This practice matters because overriding keeps one shared method name while allowing subclass-specific output.
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.hint: Read the task "Override label() in two subclasses" and identify the required result.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.title: Use one service with mixed subclasses
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.prompt: You are building a catalog report service. `Book` and `Movie` classes are provided and both define `label()`. In `services/report_service.py`, complete `build_report(items)` so it returns a list of labels by looping through the mixed item list and calling `label()` on each object. In `main.py`, print each returned label on its own line for `Book("Dune")` and `Movie("Arrival")`. This practice matters because polymorphism lets one service work with different subclasses through a shared method name.
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.hint: Read the task "Use one service with mixed subclasses" and identify the required result.

ci-inheritance-shared-price-method.title: Share a discount method through inheritance
ci-inheritance-shared-price-method.prompt: You are updating prices in a shop catalog. In `models/base_item.py`, complete `CatalogItem` so it stores `title` and `price`, and add `discounted_price(percent)` that returns the reduced price. In `models/book.py`, make `Book` inherit from `CatalogItem` and add `author`. In `main.py`, create `Book("Clean Code", 30, "Robert C. Martin")` and print the result of `discounted_price(20)`. This matters because shared business rules belong in the base class instead of being copied into every subclass.
ci-inheritance-shared-price-method.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

ci-inheritance-mixed-count-service.title: Count mixed catalog items with one shared method
ci-inheritance-mixed-count-service.prompt: You are building a quick inventory summary. `Book` and `Movie` should both inherit from `CatalogItem`, and `CatalogItem` should define `is_available()` that returns `True` when `stock > 0`. In `services/inventory_service.py`, complete `count_available(items)` so it returns how many objects in the mixed list are available. In `main.py`, use `Book("Dune", 3)` and `Movie("Arrival", 0)` and print the count. This matters because one shared base method lets a service work across different subclasses.
ci-inheritance-mixed-count-service.hint: Give both subclasses the same inherited availability rule, then count how many return `True`.

help.concept: A shared base-class method creates a common contract for subclasses. Services can then loop through mixed objects and call the same method on each one.
help.hint_1: `is_available()` should check the stored `stock` value in the base class.
help.hint_2: In the service function, increase the count only when `item.is_available()` is true.

sc-inheritance-main-benefit.title: Choose the main benefit of inheritance here
sc-inheritance-main-benefit.prompt: In a catalog app, both `Book` and `Movie` need the same `title` setup and the same `summary()` method. Why is a base class the best fit?
sc-inheritance-main-benefit.hint: Look for the choice that reduces repeated code across related classes.

help.concept: Inheritance is most useful when related classes share the same core data or behavior and should reuse one implementation instead of duplicating it.
help.hint_1: Think about what happens if you copy the same constructor and method into several classes.
help.hint_2: The strongest answer is about centralizing shared behavior so changes happen in one place.

options.a: It lets unrelated classes share random variable names.
options.b: It stores shared setup and behavior once so subclasses can reuse it.
options.c: It forces every subclass to print the exact same output forever.
options.d: It removes the need to create objects from subclasses.

sc-super-purpose.title: Identify what super() does in a subclass constructor
sc-super-purpose.prompt: A `Book` subclass has `__init__(self, title, author)` and calls `super().__init__(title)`. What is that call doing?
sc-super-purpose.hint: It reuses the parent class constructor work for the shared attribute.

help.concept: `super()` gives a subclass access to parent behavior, especially useful in constructors when the parent already handles shared attributes.
help.hint_1: Focus on which class is responsible for setting `title`.
help.hint_2: The call runs the parent constructor so the subclass does not have to rewrite the shared setup.

options.a: It creates a second Book object automatically.
options.b: It deletes subclass attributes before setup starts.
options.c: It runs the parent constructor to initialize inherited data.
options.d: It converts the subclass into a base class.

mc-polymorphism-shared-methods.title: Pick the statements that show polymorphism
mc-polymorphism-shared-methods.prompt: Which statements correctly describe polymorphism in a catalog service? Choose all that apply.
mc-polymorphism-shared-methods.hint: Look for ideas about one loop or service calling the same method on different subclasses.

help.concept: Polymorphism lets code work with different subclasses through a shared method name or base-class contract, so the caller does not need separate logic for each type.
help.hint_1: A mixed list is a clue, but only if the objects share a method the service can call.
help.hint_2: Correct choices describe one service using the same method name across different subclasses, even when each subclass returns different results.

options.a: A report service loops through `Book` and `Movie` objects and calls `label()` on each.
options.b: Each subclass can return a different result from `label()` while the service code stays the same.
options.c: Polymorphism means every subclass must have identical method bodies.
options.d: A mixed list of subclasses can be processed through one shared method name.

dr-subclass-constructor-order.title: Order the steps in a subclass constructor
dr-subclass-constructor-order.prompt: Put these steps in the usual order for a subclass constructor that reuses parent setup and then adds its own attribute.
dr-subclass-constructor-order.hint: Start with the subclass definition, then reuse the parent setup before adding subclass-specific data.

help.concept: A subclass constructor usually receives all needed values, calls the parent constructor for shared attributes, and then sets its own extra attributes.
help.hint_1: The parent setup should happen before the subclass stores its extra field.
help.hint_2: Think of the flow as: define subclass constructor, run shared setup, then add subclass-only state.

fb-inheritance-class-header.title: Complete the subclass header
fb-inheritance-class-header.prompt: Fill in the missing parent class name.
fb-inheritance-class-header.hint: The name in parentheses is the class being inherited from.

help.concept: A subclass names its parent class in parentheses so Python knows which class to inherit from.
help.hint_1: `Book` should inherit from the shared base class used for catalog items.
help.hint_2: Choose the class name that would hold common item behavior like `title` and shared methods.

fb-overriding-method-name.title: Complete the overridden method call
fb-overriding-method-name.prompt: Fill in the method name that a report service should call on both subclasses.
fb-overriding-method-name.hint: Use the shared method name from the the missing term examples.

help.concept: Polymorphism depends on different subclasses responding to the same method name, so the service can call one method consistently.
help.hint_1: The method returns text like `Book: Dune` or `Movie: Arrival`.
help.hint_2: Choose the method name used for subclass-specific display text in the examples.

sketch-0.title: Why use a base class for shared behavior?
sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
When two classes have the same core data and methods, copying that code into both classes makes your project harder to maintain. **Inheritance** lets you put the shared behavior in one base class, then let subclasses reuse it.

Imagine a catalog app with books and movies. Both need a title and a way to show a summary. Instead of writing the same constructor twice, you can move the shared parts into `CatalogItem`.

```python
class CatalogItem:
    def __init__(self, title):
        self.title = title

    def summary(self):
        return f"Item: {self.title}"

class Book(CatalogItem):
    pass
```

Step by step:

- `CatalogItem` is the base class.
- Its `__init__` method stores `title`.
- Its `summary()` method gives every item a shared behavior.
- `Book(CatalogItem)` means `Book` inherits from `CatalogItem`.
- `pass` means `Book` adds nothing yet, but it still gets the inherited constructor and method.

That means this works:

```python
book = Book("Deep Work")
print(book.summary())
```

Output:

```python
Item: Deep Work
```

The big idea is simple: put common behavior in one place, then let related subclasses reuse it.

sketch-1.title: How subclasses add their own data with super()
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
A subclass often needs **all the shared setup from the base class** plus a few extra attributes of its own. That is where `super()` helps.

```python
class CatalogItem:
    def __init__(self, title):
        self.title = title

class Book(CatalogItem):
    def __init__(self, title, author):
        super().__init__(title)
        self.author = author
```

Line by line:

- `Book` inherits from `CatalogItem`.
- `Book.__init__` receives both `title` and `author`.
- `super().__init__(title)` runs the parent class constructor.
- That parent constructor creates `self.title`.
- Then `self.author = author` adds the subclass-specific attribute.

Now the object has both pieces of data:

```python
book = Book("Deep Work", "Cal Newport")
print(book.title)
print(book.author)
```

Output:

```python
Deep Work
Cal Newport
```

Using `super()` avoids rewriting the parent setup by hand. If the shared setup changes later, you only update it in the base class.

sketch-2.title: Overriding a shared method
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
Sometimes subclasses share a method name, but each subclass needs a different version of that behavior. That is called **overriding**.

```python
class CatalogItem:
    def __init__(self, title):
        self.title = title

    def label(self):
        return self.title

class Book(CatalogItem):
    def label(self):
        return f"Book: {self.title}"

class Movie(CatalogItem):
    def label(self):
        return f"Movie: {self.title}"
```

What changed?

- The base class defines a general `label()` method.
- `Book` replaces that method with a book-specific version.
- `Movie` replaces it with a movie-specific version.
- All three classes still use the same method name: `label`.

Example:

```python
book = Book("Dune")
movie = Movie("Arrival")
print(book.label())
print(movie.label())
```

Output:

```python
Book: Dune
Movie: Arrival
```

This is useful because other parts of your program can call `label()` on different item types and still get the right result for each subclass.

Line by line: read each statement from top to bottom. Notice which names are created, which values are passed into functions, and what output the code is meant to show.

sketch-3.title: Shared method names enable polymorphism
sketch-3.bodyMarkdown:
When different subclasses all respond to the same method name, a service can work with a **mixed list of objects**. This is the practical value of inheritance and overriding.

```python
class CatalogItem:
    def __init__(self, title):
        self.title = title

class Book(CatalogItem):
    def label(self):
        return f"Book: {self.title}"

class Movie(CatalogItem):
    def label(self):
        return f"Movie: {self.title}"

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Arrival")]

for item in items:
    print(item.label())
```

Step by step:

- `items` contains different subclasses.
- The loop does not need separate code for books and movies.
- It just calls `item.label()` each time.
- Python uses the correct subclass method for each object.

Output:

```python
Book: Dune
Movie: Arrival
```

This is **polymorphism** in action: one loop, one method call, different subclass behavior. In real projects, this keeps service code shorter and easier to extend.
